title: Complete rewrite of FCFS scheduling content — hooks, intuition, WHY steps, summary
description: FCFS scores 5/36 (Grade F). Zero teaching value beyond visualization. Rewrite: HOOK: 'Imagine a coffee shop with one barista...' INTUITION: 'Like a single-lane drive-through — once served, nobody cuts in.' WHY STEPS: 'FCFS selected P1 because it arrived first. P1 will run uninterrupted.' 'P2 has been waiting since t=1 — this is the Convoy Effect.' COMPLEXITY: 'O(n) time but terrible waiting time. Watch comparison with SJF.' REAL-WORLD: 'Print queues, batch jobs, ticket systems.' SUMMARY: '3 bullets: arrival order, Convoy Effect, best for uniform jobs.'
acceptanceCriteria: FCFS concept panel opens with problem-first hook (not definition)
Coffee shop or equivalent analogy explains the concept before any technical detail
Every step description includes WHY this process was chosen
Convoy Effect explained and demonstrated
Complexity explained with concrete comparison
3-bullet summary visible in panel
Real-world applications listed

title: Complete rewrite of SJF scheduling content — WHY steps explain shortest-first selection
description: SJF scores 5/36 (Grade F). Rewrite: HOOK: 'What if the barista always serves the fastest order first? Espresso before latte.' INTUITION: 'SJF minimizes total wait by running short jobs first — mathematically optimal for average wait time.' WHY STEPS: 'SJF picked P2 because its burst (3) is shortest among ready processes.' REAL-WORLD: 'CPU burst estimation in real schedulers. Aging to prevent starvation.'
acceptanceCriteria: Hook explains why SJF is interesting (provably optimal average wait time)
Steps explain WHY SJF chose each process
Starvation problem explained: long jobs may never run
SRTF (preemptive variant) accessible with toggle and explained
Comparison with FCFS on same data shows dramatic difference

title: Complete rewrite of Round Robin content — quantum intuition and preemption explanation
description: Round Robin scores 6/36 (Grade F). Rewrite: HOOK: 'How do you share one CPU among 10 programs so each feels responsive?' INTUITION: 'Everyone gets a time slice (quantum). Like a round-table discussion: each person speaks for 2 minutes, then passes.' WHY STEPS: 'P1 preempted after quantum=2 — RR guarantees fairness by limiting each process to a fixed time slice before the next gets a turn.' EDGE CASE: 'What happens with quantum=1 vs quantum=100? Try the slider.'
acceptanceCriteria: Quantum slider visible and explained
Steps explain WHY preemption occurs after quantum expires
Edge case: quantum=1 (maximum fairness, maximum context switch overhead) vs quantum=large (degrades to FCFS)
Context switch overhead concept introduced
Real-world: 'Time-sharing systems, interactive computing, Linux CFS uses dynamic quantum'

title: Complete rewrite of MLFQ content — hospital ER analogy, progressive revelation
description: MLFQ scores 6/36 (Grade F). Rewrite: HOOK: 'Your OS runs interactive AND batch programs — how to satisfy both without knowing which is which?' INTUITION: 'Hospital ER with 3 queues — Emergency→Urgent→Routine. Everyone starts in Emergency. If your treatment takes too long, you're moved to Urgent.' PROGRESSIVE: Build from simple 2-queue model, add demotion, add I/O boost, add priority boost. REAL-WORLD: 'macOS/Windows use MLFQ. Linux uses CFS.'
acceptanceCriteria: Opens with 'how to satisfy interactive AND batch' hook
Hospital ER analogy in description panel
Progressive reveal: start simple, add demotion, add I/O boost, add priority boost
Steps explain CPU-bound punishment philosophy
I/O boost explained: 'Interactive processes release CPU early and are rewarded by staying at high priority'
Real-world comparison: MLFQ vs CFS

title: Complete rewrite of page replacement content — bookshelf analogy, WHY steps for eviction
description: All 4 page replacement algorithms score 6/36 (Grade F). Rewrite: HOOK: 'Your computer has 8GB of RAM but apps want 32GB. Which pages do you keep in memory?' INTUITION: 'A bookshelf with 3 slots. When it's full and you get a new book, which do you remove? FIFO removes the one you put there first. LRU removes the one you haven't touched in the longest time. Optimal removes the one you won't need soonest.' WHY: 'LRU evicts page 3 because it was accessed longest ago — the principle of temporal locality suggests recently used pages are likely to be used again.'
acceptanceCriteria: Shared bookshelf analogy for all page replacement algorithms
Each algorithm's eviction logic explained in WHY terms
FIFO: 'Removes oldest loaded page, regardless of recent use'
LRU: 'Removes least recently accessed — exploits temporal locality'
Optimal: 'Removes the page not needed soonest — requires future knowledge'
Clock: 'Approximates LRU cheaply using a reference bit'
Temporal locality concept explained

title: Upgrade Belady's Anomaly from C to A — add prediction, custom input, and memorable summary
description: Belady's is the best concept (18/36, Grade C) but can be world-class. Missing: self-test ('Predict: will 4 frames have more or fewer faults?'), custom reference string input (currently hardcoded), real-world connection ('This is why Linux uses LRU approximation, not FIFO'), memorable summary.
acceptanceCriteria: Prediction prompt before showing 4-frame result: 'Will more frames help?'
Custom reference string input for exploring Belady's on user data
Real-world note: 'Linux uses LRU approximation specifically because FIFO has this anomaly'
3-bullet summary: 'FIFO can have MORE faults with MORE frames. LRU/Optimal are immune. Reason: inclusion property.'

title: Complete rewrite of deadlock content — bridge analogy, 4 conditions, prevention strategies
description: RAG scores 4/36, Banker's scores 4/36 (both Grade F). Rewrite: HOOK: 'Two cars on a one-lane bridge from opposite directions — neither can back up, neither can proceed. That is deadlock.' INTUITION: 'Deadlock needs 4 conditions simultaneously: mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait. Remove any one → no deadlock.' NAME ANALOGY: 'Banker\'s Algorithm is named after a banker who only lends if they can guarantee ALL loans can eventually be repaid.'
acceptanceCriteria: Bridge/traffic analogy in opening
4 Coffman conditions listed and individually explained
Each condition linked to a prevention strategy
Banker's Algorithm explained through its namesake analogy
Steps explain safe-sequence logic: 'P0 can finish because its remaining need fits within available resources'
Deadlock vs Livelock vs Starvation distinction

title: Complete rewrite of virtual memory content — library analogy, TLB as sticky notes
description: Virtual Memory scores 3/36 (Grade F). Rewrite: HOOK: 'Every program thinks it has 16 exabytes of memory to itself. Your laptop has 16 GB. How? Virtual memory — the most important illusion in computing.' INTUITION: 'Library with 1M books, 100 shelves. Active books on shelves (RAM), rest in basement (disk). Catalog = page table. Sticky notes of 5 most recent books = TLB.' WHY: 'TLB hit means we skip the slow page table lookup — like checking your sticky notes instead of walking to the catalog.'
acceptanceCriteria: Library analogy in description panel
TLB as 'sticky notes' / 'speed dial' analogy
Page fault explained: 'book not on shelf, must fetch from basement (disk) — takes 1000x longer'
Steps explain WHY TLB hit is fast vs page table miss
Address breakdown visualized: virtual address → page number + offset
Real-world: 'Every program you run right now is using virtual memory'

title: Complete rewrite of memory allocation content — parking lot analogy, fragmentation intuition
description: All 3 allocation algorithms score 6/36 (Grade F). Rewrite: HOOK: 'Imagine a parking lot where cars leave gaps. How do you fit new cars without wasting space?' INTUITION: 'First Fit: park in the first spot that fits. Best Fit: find the tightest spot. Worst Fit: find the biggest spot (leaves room for others).' WHY: 'First Fit chose this block because it scans from the start and takes the first one large enough — fast but can create small unusable gaps at the beginning.'
acceptanceCriteria: Parking lot analogy in description
Each strategy explained through the analogy
Fragmentation explained: 'small gaps that are individually too small to use but collectively waste significant memory'
Internal vs external fragmentation distinguished
Steps explain WHY each algorithm chose a specific block
Real-world: 'Linux kernel uses Buddy System for page-frame allocation, slab allocator for small objects'

title: Complete rewrite of thread sync content — bathroom analogy, ownership distinction
description: All 3 sync primitives score 3/36 (Grade F). Rewrite: HOOK: 'Two threads both try to update your bank balance at the same time. One adds $100, one subtracts $50. Without synchronization, you might end up with the wrong balance.' INTUITION: 'Mutex = bathroom lock (one person at a time, only the person inside can unlock). Semaphore = parking garage with N spaces (counter, anyone can signal). RW Lock = library (many readers, but writing requires exclusive access).' KEY DISTINCTION: 'Mutex has OWNERSHIP — only the locking thread can unlock. This is NOT the same as a binary semaphore.'
acceptanceCriteria: Bank balance race condition as hook
Bathroom/parking garage/library analogies for each primitive
OWNERSHIP distinction for mutex explicitly called out
Steps include: 'T0 acquires the mutex — now ONLY T0 can release it (ownership)'
Common mistake: 'A mutex is NOT a binary semaphore — the ownership matters'
When to use each: decision guide
Real-world: 'Go sync.Mutex, Java synchronized, Python threading.Lock'

title: Add prediction/self-test mode to all step-through concepts
description: T11 (self-test) scores 0/3 across ALL concepts. Add a 'Predict Mode' toggle that pauses before each step and asks: 'Which process will run next?' (scheduling), 'Will this be a hit or fault?' (page replacement), 'Which page will be evicted?' (page replacement). Student selects answer, then sees if they're right. Research shows prediction improves learning by 40% (Mayer's active learning principle).
acceptanceCriteria: Toggle for 'Predict Mode' in step controls
Before each step, a prediction prompt with 2-4 options
Immediate feedback: correct (green check) or incorrect (red X with explanation)
Score tracker: 'You predicted 7/10 correctly'
Works for: scheduling (which process next), page replacement (hit/fault + which evicted), memory (TLB hit/fault)

title: Add complexity intuition text and interactive counter to all algorithms
description: T5 (complexity intuition) scores 0/3 across ALL concepts. No algorithm shows complexity. Add: visible operation counter during step-through (comparisons, swaps, page faults), complexity badge (O(n), O(n²)), and intuition text: 'Double the input size and watch the counter — O(n²) means the work QUADRUPLES.'
acceptanceCriteria: Operation counter visible during step-through for all algorithms
Complexity badge (O notation) on each algorithm in dropdown
Intuition text: what the O notation MEANS in concrete terms
Challenge: 'Try 5 processes, then 10. Notice the counter — is it 2x or 4x more operations?'
Comparison view shows operation counts side-by-side

title: Add edge case demonstrations to all concepts
description: T6 (edge cases) scores 0/3 across all concepts except Belady's (2/3). Add preset 'Edge Case' buttons: scheduling: 'All arrive at t=0', 'One giant process', 'All equal burst'. Page replacement: 'Sequential access', 'Repeated access', 'Random thrashing'. Memory alloc: 'Worst-case fragmentation', 'Perfect fit'. Thread sync: 'Maximum contention'.
acceptanceCriteria: Each concept has 2-3 preset 'Edge Case' buttons
Clicking loads the edge case data and runs automatically
Brief explanation of why this edge case is interesting
Scheduling: 'All arrive at t=0 — FCFS and SJF produce different results'
Page replacement: 'Sequential scan — FIFO performs poorly, LRU is no better'

title: Add GC visualizer teaching content — explain WHY GC matters, not just WHAT happens
description: GC visualizer scores 9/36 (Grade F) despite excellent visuals. Missing: WHY different GC algorithms exist (throughput vs latency tradeoff), INTUITION (G1=region cleanup crew, ZGC=concurrent invisible janitor), REAL-WORLD ('Choose G1 for general workloads, ZGC for latency-sensitive APIs, Shenandoah for large heaps').
acceptanceCriteria: WHY section: 'GC matters because memory leaks crash servers and GC pauses cause latency spikes'
Throughput vs Latency tradeoff explained
Each GC algorithm has intuitive description beyond current technical text
Decision guide: when to choose G1 vs ZGC vs Shenandoah
Real-world: 'Discord migrated to ZGC for sub-ms pauses; Netflix uses G1 for most services'

title: Create OS Module Content Style Guide for all future content
description: The ONE CHANGE that would improve everything: a mandatory content template. Every concept must have: (1) Hook — why should I care? (2) Analogy — concrete intuition before formalism (3) WHY steps — every step explains the algorithm's reasoning (4) Complexity intuition — what O(n²) MEANS (5) Edge case — preset demonstrating interesting behavior (6) Real-world — where this is used in production (7) Common mistake — what students get wrong (8) Summary — 3-bullet flashcard content. Write this as a CONTRIBUTING.md section for content developers.
acceptanceCriteria: Content style guide document created in docs/ or CONTRIBUTING.md
Template with all 8 required sections
2 example concepts fully written to the template (FCFS + LRU)
Checklist for content reviewers: 'Does this concept have all 8 sections?'
Minimum quality bar: 'No concept ships below Grade C (18/36)'
